metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete 'ticket-store generation token' — specify whether it is the tracker branch HEAD SHA, a snapshot of per-ticket dir-hashes, or a new primitive — and verify that the chosen mechanism detects mid-window in-process writes before a git commit lands.
evidence: 're-collect all dimensions outside the lock with a ticket-store generation token'
'verify the generation is unchanged'
ABSENCE: No definition of what a 'ticket-store generation token' is in the plan or in any existing src/rebar/_store/*.py, src/rebar/reducer/*.py module.
Existing store has write lock + dir-hash-based reducer cache (_cache.py _REDUCER_CACHE_VERSION + stat-based dir_hash), but no generation counter or OCC token primitive.
git commit SHA on the tickets branch is an established OCC mechanism for git-backed stores (used in git itself), but the plan does not select or evaluate it.
findings.finding: The plan's pre-sign OCC mechanism asserts 'a ticket-store generation token' that does not exist in the codebase. The plan says: 're-collect all dimensions outside the lock with a ticket-store generation token. Acquire the global write lock, verify the generation is unchanged.' No such token is defined anywhere in the store primitives (`_store/lock.py`, `reducer/_cache.py`, `reducer/_api.py`). The existing store offers: (a) the write lock for mutual exclusion, (b) dir-hash for reducer-cache invalidation, and (c) git commit SHAs on the tickets branch. The plan never resolves which of these (or a new primitive) serves as the 'generation token', nor whether a diff between pre-LLM and post-LLM snapshots of multiple tickets' dir-hashes is the intended mechanism. This is a novel OCC pattern applied to a git-backed event-sourced store that has prior art (git's own atomic commit boundary as a per-ticket-dir mtime/inode snapshot, or the tracker branch HEAD SHA) — none of which are evaluated or selected.
findings.location: Settled design § Signed plan-material pins — 'The phase story owns one unified pre-sign transaction … re-collect all dimensions outside the lock with a ticket-store generation token. Acquire the global write lock, verify the generation is unchanged'
scenarios: An implementor must invent the generation token mechanism from scratch, potentially choosing dir-hash comparison (expensive for multi-ticket) or branch-HEAD SHA (may not detect mid-directory changes between commits), leading to a drift window that defeats the OCC guarantee.
If the token is the tracker branch HEAD SHA, concurrent in-process writes (before a git commit) will be invisible to it, making the OCC check ineffective within a single session.
findings.suggested_fix: The simplest grounded option: define the generation token as a dict of `{ticket_id: dir_hash}` for the subject ticket and each pinned related ticket, captured before the LLM call using the existing `reducer/_cache.py` dir-hash computation. Under the write lock, recompute these dir-hashes and compare; abort if any diverged. This reuses an existing primitive and avoids a new git operation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs to 75de (compute_plan_review_validity) explicitly requiring phase-incompatible attestations to be rejected at each validity-check call site (claim, close, reuse, re-sign), and add a matching AC to 2bbd confirming the close gate surfaces a phase-mismatch error.
evidence: 75de implementation: 'The close profile sets check_code_freshness=false while retaining signature, registry, phase, own-material, and configured pin checks' — 'phase' is listed but absent from ACs
75de ACs: all seven enumerate 'pin drift' scenarios only; no AC covers phase-compatibility rejection at validity-check time
5967 ACs: 'Planning mode rejects execution-phase attestations' / 'Execution mode accepts planning attestations' — scoped to signing, not validity evaluation
2bbd AC: 'Enabled close enforcement accepts only certified, phase-compatible, material-and-pin-current reviews' — relies on 75de profile but 75de has no matching AC
Parent design: 'Close checks signature, compatible phase, the ticket's own material, relationship pins' — compatible phase is a distinct close requirement
findings.finding: Phase-compatibility enforcement at validity-check time (claim, close, reuse) is not covered by any child's acceptance criteria. 75de owns compute_plan_review_validity and its implementation text says the close profile retains a 'phase' check, but 75de's ACs only enumerate pin-drift invalidation scenarios. 5967's phase-compatibility ACs ('Planning mode rejects execution-phase attestations', 'Execution mode accepts planning/execution attestations') are scoped to the review-signing path, not the post-sign validity evaluation that claim, close, and reuse perform. 2bbd delegates to 75de ('Call the validity story's compute_plan_review_validity close profile') and asserts 'Enabled close enforcement accepts only certified, phase-compatible reviews', but no AC in 75de or 2bbd verifies that an attestation signed with an incompatible phase is actually rejected at close/claim/reuse time.
findings.location: child 75de-02b0-d454-410b (compute_plan_review_validity close profile ACs) and child 2bbd-87f6-8765-4b00 (close gate AC)
scenarios: A planning-mode ticket is closed with a stale execution-phase attestation; compute_plan_review_validity's phase check is never unit-tested and the incompatibility is silently accepted
An attestation reuse path (not close) accepts a mismatched phase because only 5967's signing-time ACs were implemented and no validity-time phase AC drives the reuse test
findings.suggested_fix: In 75de's AC list add: 'compute_plan_review_validity rejects an attestation whose signed review-phase is incompatible with the ticket's current execution mode at every consumer call site (claim, close, reuse, re-sign, audit).' In 2bbd's AC list add: 'Close returns a phase-incompatibility error and review-plan remediation when the existing attestation's phase is incompatible, even when pin enforcement is disabled.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the 'generation token' in the pre-sign transaction is concretely a snapshot of per-ticket dir_hashes (subject + all related tickets) taken before the LLM call and compared after — not a new global counter — and specify that implementers should reuse `compute_dir_hash` from `reducer/_cache.py` for this purpose.
evidence: Plan phrase: 'Acquire the global write lock, verify the generation is unchanged, compare the fresh snapshot with the pre-LLM snapshot'
Codebase: src/rebar/reducer/_cache.py `compute_dir_hash` and `read_cache` — change detection is per-ticket-directory dir_hash (filename+size+mtime), not a global store-generation counter
ABSENCE: no 'generation_token', 'store_generation', or equivalent global counter exists in src/rebar/
findings.finding: The plan describes a 'ticket-store generation token' as the change-detection primitive for the pre-sign retry loop, but no such global store-generation counter exists in the codebase. The actual mechanism is per-ticket dir_hashes (filename+size+mtime in `reducer/_cache.py`). The plan's language implies a single token covering subject + all related tickets, but the implementation would require snapshotting N independent per-ticket dir_hashes. An implementer searching for a 'generation token' API will not find one, and the discrepancy could cause them to either add unnecessary infrastructure or silently use an incorrect mechanism.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins: 'The phase story owns one unified pre-sign transaction... Acquire the global write lock, verify the generation is unchanged...'
scenarios: Implementer creates a new global counter or database row to represent a generation token, adding unnecessary infrastructure

Implementer uses a per-ticket dir_hash correctly but the plan's ambiguous 'generation token' language causes confusion about whether the multi-ticket snapshot is a single value or a dict of per-ticket hashes
The retry check is implemented only for the subject ticket's dir_hash, missing drift in related tickets during the LLM call
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a ticket-store generation token' with 'a per-ticket dir_hash snapshot ({ticket_id: dir_hash} for subject and all related tickets, computed via `compute_dir_hash`)'. Specify that the lock-held verification step re-computes each dir_hash and compares them to the pre-LLM snapshot.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact mechanism by which the manifest signature scheme tolerates unknown lines in older readers (e.g., sign only the known-field prefix, use a hash-then-append structure, or document that the existing scheme already canonicalizes only known fields), so the backward-compatibility and rollback claims are verifiably grounded.
evidence: "readers predating this feature ignore unknown lines and therefore treat the attestation like a legacy unpinned review"
"Pins are additive manifest metadata"

The manifest is described as a deterministic, signed document; signature verification requires re-computing a hash over the same bytes the signer used. If old readers omit unknown lines from their hash input, the hash diverges. The plan does not specify whether the signature scheme hashes only known fields, hashes the full text, or uses a scheme that tolerates unknown lines (e.g., a canonical serialization that old readers can reproduce without knowing about pin lines).

ABSENCE: No specification of how the hash/HMAC is computed relative to unknown manifest lines, nor any mention of a canonical serialization approach (e.g., hash-then-append, or sign only known fields) that would make the claimed backward compatibility hold.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Older readers ignore additive pin lines without invalidating the signature' as the coexistence guarantee, but does not specify the mechanism by which an older reader can skip unknown manifest lines and still reproduce the correct signature hash. If the signature is computed over the full serialized manifest (including pin lines), an older reader that omits those lines when re-computing the hash will produce a mismatched digest and fail verification — the opposite of the claimed backward compatibility. This underspecifies the T12 coexistence requirement for rolling deployments (e.g., remote MCP server with multiple instances at mixed versions).
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins — 'Older readers ignore additive pin lines without invalidating the signature' and 'readers predating this feature ignore unknown lines and therefore treat the attestation like a legacy unpinned review.'
scenarios: Remote MCP server with a rolling deploy: some instances upgraded (now emitting and reading pin lines), others still on old code. An old-code instance attempts to verify a new-code-produced attestation, re-computes the manifest hash without pin lines, gets a digest mismatch, and rejects the attestation as invalid — breaking the claimed coexistence guarantee.

Operational rollback: code is rolled back but newly produced attestations (with pin lines) remain in the store. Rolled-back readers attempt to verify these attestations, fail signature checks, and surface spurious invalid-attestation errors requiring manual remediation — contradicting the 'no data cleanup' rollback claim.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection under 'Signed plan-material pins' clarifying the signing model: for example, 'The manifest hash is computed over only the fields the signing reader knows; unknown lines are appended after the signature line and not covered by the hash' (an append-after-signature approach), OR 'The existing manifest parser already skips unrecognized lines before hashing, and pin lines are designed to match that skip pattern.' Either choice closes the gap; the plan should name which one applies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each new manifest parser (`manifest_review_phase()`, `manifest_priority_floor()`, `manifest_pins()`), specify the exact error-handling contract: what the parser returns on a malformed line (e.g., raise `MalformedManifestError`, return a sentinel, or treat as legacy-absent), and whether a malformed line makes the whole attestation invalid or only the relevant field.
evidence: 'Add manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), and manifest_pins() parsers beside existing manifest parsers; every lifecycle consumer uses them.'
'missing, nonnumeric, or range-invalid execution floors are malformed' — consequence for parser is not stated
'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' — no error grammar for extra/missing tokens
findings.finding: The plan names three new manifest parsers (`manifest_review_phase()`, `manifest_priority_floor()`, `manifest_pins()`) as new code artifacts implementers must add, but provides no error-handling grammar for the `plan-material-pin` and `priority-floor` manifest lines beyond the happy path. For `priority-floor`, the plan specifies what values are invalid ('missing, nonnumeric, or range-invalid execution floors are malformed') but does not specify the parser's behavior on a malformed line: does it raise, return None, or treat the attestation as legacy-unpinned? The same gap applies to a `plan-material-pin` line with a missing or extra field token.
findings.location: Settled design → Signed plan-material pins (manifest parsers paragraph) and Execution reviews paragraph
scenarios: A `priority-floor: abc` line causes one parser implementation to raise and another to return None, leading to divergent attestation-validity results across consumers.
A pin line with only two tokens is treated as legacy (ignored) by one consumer and as malformed (invalid) by another.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether compaction snapshots containing the new `plan_review_phase` field are forward-compatible with pre-upgrade code (e.g., unknown fields are silently ignored), or add a rollback step that invalidates the reducer cache to force recomputation from raw events.
evidence: 'A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction'
'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten.'
No explicit statement on old-code forward-compatibility of new compaction snapshots containing plan_review_phase.
findings.finding: The rollback path for the compiled-state schema change (new plan_review_phase field in compaction snapshots) is not explicitly addressed. The plan names 'disable enforcement flags + code rollback' as the operational rollback, and notes a reducer-cache version bump triggers recomputation, but does not state what happens to already-written compaction snapshots when old code (post-rollback) encounters them — whether unknown fields are silently dropped, cause parse errors, or require snapshot invalidation.
findings.location: Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate — plan_review_phase projection; 'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback'
scenarios: Team rolls back code after snapshots with plan_review_phase have been written to the store; old reducer code encounters unknown field and either panics, ignores it silently, or produces incorrect compiled state — the plan gives no explicit guarantee.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the rollback path: 'On code rollback, bump the reducer-cache version again (or clear the cache) so that old code recomputes compiled state from raw events, ignoring the new snapshot field; compaction snapshots written by the new code are not re-read by old code until cache is cleared.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state for compaction snapshots containing `plan_review_phase`: specify whether pre-feature reducer code safely ignores the field (forward-compatible snapshot schema), or document the recovery procedure (e.g., snapshot re-reduction from raw events) if it does not.
evidence: "Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten."
"plan_review_phase projection … carried in compiled state and compaction snapshots"
Rollback section covers attestation manifests (pin lines) explicitly as forward-compatible but makes no equivalent assertion for the plan_review_phase field in compaction snapshots.
ABSENCE: no statement that pre-feature reducer code ignores unknown fields in compaction snapshots, or that snapshots are re-reducible from raw events after rollback without the new field.
findings.finding: The rollback section asserts flag-disabling as the back-out path and states 'no stored attestation must be rewritten,' but it never asserts the schema state of compaction snapshots after rollback. Compaction snapshots will persistently carry the new `plan_review_phase` field once written; if older (pre-feature) reducer code encounters those snapshots, the plan does not specify whether that field is safely ignored, causes reducer errors, or is silently dropped — leaving an ambiguous and potentially broken post-rollback schema state.
findings.location: Settled design > Enforcement and rollout — 'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten.'
scenarios: Operator disables enforcement flags and rolls back code while some tickets already have compaction snapshots carrying plan_review_phase; older reducer code either errors on the unknown field or silently drops it, yielding incorrect phase derivation with no specified recovery path.
A partial rollout (some nodes on new code, some on old) reads mixed compaction snapshots, producing inconsistent phase projections with no documented handling.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the rollback clause: 'Compaction snapshots carrying plan_review_phase are forward-compatible: pre-feature reducer code treats unknown projection fields as absent and recomputes from events if needed, so no snapshot rewrite is required on rollback.' If that guarantee cannot be made, document a compensating step (e.g., triggering re-reduction from the raw event log for affected tickets before serving traffic on the rolled-back code).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the escalation predicate for prerequisite-criterion `indeterminate` records: specify whether the resulting unsigned INDETERMINATE verdict carries a `coverage.resolution_class` (and if so, which value), whether it is retryable, and how it is distinguished from systemic LLM-degrade indeterminate in the verdict shape consumers read.
evidence: 'indeterminate is used only when specialized model evaluation, output parsing, or attribution cannot complete. It never asserts a blocking incompatibility, but complete prerequisite coverage is required for certification, so any indeterminate record yields an unsigned INDETERMINATE review rather than signed PASS.'

Existing plan_review_verdict.schema.json `coverage.resolution_class` enum is for systemic LLM degrade dispositions (WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW, etc.) — it does not include a 'prerequisite-indeterminate' class
The plan does not define whether the prerequisite-indeterminate verdict is retryable (which affects exit-code logic)
findings.finding: The promotion rule from a prerequisite-criterion `indeterminate` coverage record to the top-level unsigned INDETERMINATE review verdict reuses the word 'indeterminate' for two distinct concepts without defining the escalation predicate. The plan says 'any indeterminate record yields an unsigned INDETERMINATE review' — but the boundary between an indeterminate record (LLM could not evaluate one prerequisite) and the existing top-level indeterminate verdict (systemic failure, retryable classification) is never formally distinguished. Specifically, the plan does not say whether a prerequisite-criterion indeterminate produces a `resolution_class` on the verdict's `coverage` object, or how it interacts with the existing `LLMOutcome`/`retryable` disposition machinery.
findings.location: Settled design → Prerequisite consistency review (first paragraph, indeterminate handling) and Signed plan-material pins (third bullet)
scenarios: The CLI reads `resolution_class` to decide exit code 11 (retryable) vs exit 2 (non-retryable indeterminate); a prerequisite-indeterminate without a `resolution_class` defaults to exit 2, but a model parse failure would normally produce exit 11 — the plan doesn't say which.
An audit consumer interprets a prerequisite-indeterminate as a systemic degradation when it is actually a data/attribution issue.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation deliverable to this plan: capture the signed-pin attestation model, manifest extension contract, generation-token pre-sign protocol, and prerequisite criterion isolation boundary in an ADR or AGENTS.md section before the feature ships.
evidence: 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation' — this refers to user-facing docs only, not internal architectural decision records.
ABSENCE: No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, architecture doc, or design document update anywhere in the plan.

Novel decisions include: the signed-pin attestation model, generation-token optimistic concurrency for pre-sign, natural-propagation semantics for pin invalidation, isolated prerequisite criterion architecture, manifest format extension rules, and plan_review_phase projection bootstrap logic.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions — signed plan-material pins as a new attestation primitive, a generation-token-based pre-sign transaction protocol, a new ticket lifecycle projection (plan_review_phase), a specialized isolated prerequisite criterion pipeline, and an extended manifest format — but mentions no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc capture for any of these.
findings.location: Settled design (all subsections); Health and documentation section; Not in scope
scenarios: A new contributor encounters the generation-token pre-sign transaction and cannot find the rationale for 3 retries, the exact snapshot dimensions, or the choice of natural vs. eager propagation — no ADR captures the trade-off reasoning.

A future maintainer changes the manifest parser and inadvertently breaks the additive-metadata contract (older readers ignoring unknown lines) because the contract was never recorded in AGENTS.md or a design doc.
The prerequisite criterion isolation rule (general finder/verifier never see prerequisite material) is quietly violated in a refactor because the architectural boundary was never documented.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'An ADR or AGENTS.md section documents (a) the natural-propagation vs. eager-invalidation trade-off, (b) the manifest additive-metadata contract for forward/backward compatibility, (c) the generation-token pre-sign transaction protocol and retry policy, and (d) the prerequisite criterion isolation boundary.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or implementation item to 5967 explicitly stating that the Pass-3 threshold function (max(criterion_threshold, 0.80) for execution reviews) is exposed as a named shared utility, and add a corresponding integration item to 7c27 confirming it consumes that named export.
evidence: 7c27 implementation: 'Reuse the phase story's shared Pass-3 threshold function for upheld findings, including the execution floor'
5967 implementation: 'For an execution review, use effective blocking threshold max(normal criterion threshold, 0.8)' — no mention of exposing this as a shared function
5967 ACs: none mention exporting or exposing a threshold utility for other criteria to consume
Parent plan: 'Only an upheld finding record can proceed to normal Pass 3 and the execution floor' — implies shared logic but does not mandate a named export
findings.finding: 7c27 declares it will 'Reuse the phase story's shared Pass-3 threshold function for upheld findings, including the execution floor,' creating a hard implementation dependency on 5967 exporting a named, reusable threshold function — but 5967 has no AC, no implementation line, and no interface-exposure item acknowledging this export. 5967 implements the 0.80 blocking floor internally for its own execution reviews and does not commit to making it available as a shared callable.
findings.location: child 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 (Pass-2/Pass-3 threshold reuse) and child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d (threshold implementation)
scenarios: 5967 ships its threshold logic as an internal detail; 7c27 attempts to call a function that does not exist as a public interface, causing a runtime error or requiring an unplanned refactor
Integration testing in 145e discovers the missing shared threshold only after both children are otherwise complete, requiring rework of 5967
findings.suggested_fix: In 5967's implementation section add: 'Expose apply_execution_floor(criterion_threshold) -> effective_threshold as a module-level utility callable by other criteria.' In 7c27's implementation section change 'Reuse the phase story's shared Pass-3 threshold function' to 'Call 5967's exported apply_execution_floor() utility and add an integration test asserting the correct effective threshold for execution-mode prerequisite findings.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the tail-item fixture contract (minimum count, required coverage of edge cases, and ground-truth label schema) and specify how recall/false-acceptance are computed and how the one-per-call gold baseline is captured and compared in CI.
evidence: 'Packed behavior uses the existing whole-child bin-packing pattern and is release-gated against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline: packed recall must be no lower, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite must receive a record, and attribution errors must be zero. Tail-item fixtures are mandatory.'
ABSENCE: No definition of what 'tail-item fixtures' contain, how many are required, or what ground-truth labels they carry.
ABSENCE: No definition of the metric computation methodology (what counts as a true positive, false positive, etc. for recall and false-acceptance in this context).
ABSENCE: No specification of how the one-per-call baseline numbers are captured, stored, or compared in CI.
findings.finding: AC19 ('Packed prerequisite evaluation matches the one-per-call fidelity contract') specifies a release gate comparing packed recall and false-acceptance to a one-per-call gold baseline, but the plan does not define: (a) what 'tail-item fixtures' are or how many constitute a mandatory set, (b) how recall and false-acceptance are computed (e.g., which labels are ground-truth, what unit is a 'prerequisite evaluation'), or (c) how the gold baseline numbers are established and persisted. An implementing agent cannot construct the gate, write the fixtures, or verify the criterion without resolving these gaps.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC19; Settled design § Prerequisite consistency review — 'release-gated against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline'
scenarios: An executor writes a test harness but guesses at fixture content, making the gate trivially passable with any two fixtures.
An executor cannot determine what 'recall' means for prerequisite disposition records (is indeterminate a miss? is finding a hit?) without a ground-truth label schema.
CI comparison fails silently if the baseline isn't persisted — executor must decide where to store it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection or annotation to the 'Packed behavior' paragraph specifying: (1) ground-truth labels per fixture (e.g., each fixture names which prerequisite IDs should produce 'finding' vs 'consistent'); (2) metric definitions (recall = upheld finding records / ground-truth finding prerequisites; false acceptance = consistent records where ground-truth is finding); (3) baseline persistence (e.g., a checked-in JSON snapshot produced by a one-per-call run that CI diffs against); (4) minimum fixture count covering at least one tail-position item and one bin-boundary item.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC 4 with the three-retry design: reword it to 'Pre-sign re-collection retries up to three times on concurrent change, then returns an unsigned retryable result' (or, if abort-on-first is the real intent, remove the retry description from Settled Design).
evidence: AC: 'Pre-sign re-collection aborts after any concurrent relationship or fingerprint change.'
Settled Design: 'If generation changed, release, re-collect, and retry at most three times; exhaustion returns a retryable unsigned result.'
findings.finding: AC 4 says 'Pre-sign re-collection aborts after any concurrent relationship or fingerprint change', implying immediate abort on first conflict, but the Settled Design section describes a three-retry loop: 'If generation changed, release, re-collect, and retry at most three times; exhaustion returns a retryable unsigned result.' These are mutually contradictory: one demands abort-on-first-change, the other demands retry-then-abort.
findings.location: AC line 4 vs Settled Design – 'The phase story owns one unified pre-sign transaction'
scenarios: Implementer writes a retry loop (following the design body) but test author writes an abort-on-first-change test (following the AC), causing a test failure or incorrect implementation.
The AC as written cannot verify the three-retry contract because it specifies the opposite behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: AC 4: 'Pre-sign re-collection retries at most three times on any concurrent relationship or fingerprint change; on exhaustion it returns an unsigned retryable result without signing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the 'rebar enables enforcement' sentence to explicitly name both flags: 'The rebar project sets verify.enforce_plan_material_pins and verify.require_plan_review_for_close to true.'
evidence: Settled Design: 'New reviews emit pins in every project regardless of enforcement configuration. The rebar project enables enforcement.'
AC 21: 'Rebar enables both opt-in enforcement flags while reusable defaults remain disabled.'
Two distinct flags defined: verify.enforce_plan_material_pins and verify.require_plan_review_for_close
findings.finding: The Settled Design body says 'The rebar project enables enforcement' (one phrase, no qualification) in the 'Enforcement and rollout' section, but AC 21 says 'Rebar enables both opt-in enforcement flags while reusable defaults remain disabled.' The body never explicitly states that require_plan_review_for_close is also enabled in rebar, leaving an implementer uncertain whether one or both flags are set.
findings.location: Settled Design – Enforcement and rollout ('The rebar project enables enforcement') vs AC 21
scenarios: Implementer sets only verify.enforce_plan_material_pins for rebar (the natural reading of the body sentence), leaving require_plan_review_for_close disabled, causing AC 21 to fail.
Reviewer interprets the body differently from the AC, leading to inconsistent implementations.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'The rebar project enables enforcement.' with 'The rebar project enables both verify.enforce_plan_material_pins and verify.require_plan_review_for_close; all other projects inherit the disabled defaults.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC covering force-close: 'Force-close with a non-empty reason bypasses the close-time plan-review gate and records an audit entry; structural child-closure rules remain unaffected.'
evidence: 'The existing force-close with a non-empty reason bypasses completion and close-time plan-review gates with an audit record. Structural child-closure rules remain non-bypassable.'
ABSENCE: No AC tests force-close bypass of the new plan-review gate or the associated audit record.
findings.finding: The Settled Design describes a distinct behavior — 'The existing force-close with a non-empty reason bypasses completion and close-time plan-review gates with an audit record' — that is new within this epic (close-time plan-review gates are new), yet no AC covers whether force-close correctly bypasses the new close-gate or records the required audit entry.
findings.location: Settled Design – Execution reviews and close gate ('The existing force-close with a non-empty reason...')
scenarios: Implementer omits the audit record for force-close bypass of the new gate because the AC set never requires it.
A regression in the force-close path silently disables the new gate for all closes without a test to catch it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'close-profile review' in AC 16 with a term that matches the plan body, e.g. 'the plan-review attestation evaluated at close time' or simply 'close-gate attestation validity'.
evidence: AC 16: 'Code freshness alone never invalidates the close-profile review.'

Settled Design: 'Close checks signature, compatible phase, the ticket's own material, relationship pins, and other non-code attestation validity, but intentionally ignores file dependency hashes and whole-HEAD freshness…'
ABSENCE: The term 'close-profile review' never appears in the plan body.
findings.finding: AC 16 uses the term 'close-profile review' ('Code freshness alone never invalidates the close-profile review') but this phrase does not appear anywhere else in the plan. The body consistently describes what is checked at close ('Close checks signature, compatible phase, the ticket's own material, relationship pins…') but never names this a 'close-profile review', making the AC's verify step reference an undefined entity.
findings.location: AC line 16 vs Settled Design – Execution reviews and close gate
scenarios: A verifier cannot determine what artifact 'close-profile review' refers to and cannot write a correct test.
The undefined term makes it ambiguous whether the AC refers to the attestation checked at close-time, or a separate review type.
findings.suggested_fix: AC 16: 'Code freshness (file-dependency hashes or whole-HEAD SHA) alone never invalidates the plan-review attestation checked at close time.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit documentation targets for the two new config flags (`verify.enforce_plan_material_pins`, `verify.require_plan_review_for_close`) and the execution-phase review contract (planning/execution modes, 0.80 priority floor) in the advanced attestation/gate documentation section, and note whether existing close-operation or attestation-validity docs need updating.
evidence: 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation.' — only pin lines are mentioned; config flags and execution-phase semantics are absent from the doc commitment.
verify.enforce_plan_material_pins and verify.require_plan_review_for_close are new config entries with non-trivial behavioral contracts but no documentation target is named.
Execution-phase semantics (planning vs. execution mode, 0.80 floor, compatible phase rules) are novel user-facing behaviors with no documentation commitment beyond the plan text itself.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly documents where plan-material-pin lives in user docs, but does not commit to documenting the two new user-visible config flags (verify.enforce_plan_material_pins, verify.require_plan_review_for_close) or the new execution-phase review semantics (execution vs. planning mode, 0.80 priority floor) in any doc or ADR. These are new operator-facing contracts that will affect how teams configure and operate the system.
findings.location: Settled design / Health and documentation section; Enforcement and rollout; Execution reviews and close gate
scenarios: An operator enabling verify.require_plan_review_for_close finds no documentation explaining which ticket types are exempt, what 'compatible phase' means, or how to interpret close errors — leading to misconfiguration or support burden.
A team member running review-plan on an in_progress ticket is surprised by the 0.80 floor behavior with no reference documentation to consult.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the 'Health and documentation' section to read: 'The two enforcement config flags, execution-phase semantics (planning/execution modes, 0.80 priority floor), and compatible-phase close-gate rules are documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation. Existing docs covering attestation validity and close operations are updated to reflect the new close-gate and phase compatibility rules.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement/spike step — e.g., run the execution floor against a representative sample of historical review findings to derive a data-grounded threshold value, or explicitly scope out a post-rollout measurement plan with stated success criteria before hardcoding 0.80 in the protocol.
evidence: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
No spike, pilot run, historical finding-distribution analysis, or fixture measuring miss-rate at different thresholds is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
ABSENCE: No 'TBD: measure X' or 'derive from fixture' language for this threshold.
findings.finding: The 0.80 execution priority-floor is asserted as protocol policy with no measurement plan, spike, or empirical derivation cited. The plan explicitly acknowledges it is 'not an empirically claimed optimum,' meaning the threshold's adequacy for risk management of in-progress work is unvalidated before build-out.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate — 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
scenarios: The 0.80 floor is too conservative, blocking valid execution-phase reviews that would have passed at 0.75, causing agent friction on legitimately low-risk work.
The 0.80 floor is too permissive, allowing marginally-risky execution plans through that a 0.85 floor would catch — defeating the stated risk management purpose.
findings.suggested_fix: Before finalizing 0.80, add a validation step: sample N completed execution-phase tickets, compute the empirical finding-score distribution, and confirm 0.80 sits at or above the Pth percentile of scores on tickets that had real blocking issues. Alternatively, treat the initial value as a named constant (e.g., EXECUTION_PRIORITY_FLOOR = 0.80) with a comment referencing the derivation issue and a follow-on measurement ticket.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expose the execution priority floor as a named project-level config (e.g., verify.execution_priority_floor defaulting to 0.80), consistent with how the two enforcement flags are handled, so threshold evolution requires only a config change rather than a code and protocol change.
evidence: 'a future required floor compares numerically, so raising policy makes older execution attestations require re-review while lowering it accepts attestations carrying the older higher floor'
The plan explicitly models future changes to the threshold, confirming it is expected to evolve.
ABSENCE: No project-config setting like verify.execution_priority_floor is mentioned; the value is described as 'fixed protocol' with no config knob.
findings.finding: The 0.80 priority-floor is expected to evolve (the plan explicitly discusses raising or lowering it) but is hardcoded as a fixed protocol value with no configuration knob, requiring a code change and manifest-protocol version bump each time the policy threshold changes.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate — 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy… a future required floor compares numerically, so raising policy makes older execution attestations require re-review'
scenarios: Policy needs to be tightened to 0.85 after observing risky execution reviews pass — requires a code change and deployment rather than a config toggle.
Different project profiles (e.g., rebar vs. a consumer project) need different execution risk tolerances — impossible without code changes since the value is protocol-level.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `verify.execution_priority_floor` with default 0.80 alongside `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` and `verify.require_plan_review_for_close`. The manifest still records the numeric value actually used at sign time, preserving the evolution-safe comparison property. Rebar sets it to 0.80 explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the plan title and Problem statement to explicitly name all three bundled feature areas (relationship-pin currency, execution-phase tracking with priority floor, and the close-gate / prerequisite-consistency review) so the headline faithfully represents the full scope of work.
evidence: Title: 'Keep plan-review attestations current across relationships and execution'
Subtitle: 'Invalidate downstream plan reviews after upstream plan-material changes'
'verify.require_plan_review_for_close defaults to false. When enabled, task, story, and epic close operations require a current certified plan review.'
'A specialized criterion runs deterministically when direct prerequisites exist. General finder and verifier calls never receive prerequisite material.'
'The signed manifest line review-phase: has exactly planning or execution. Execution reviews also carry priority-floor: 0.80.'
None of these three features appear in the title, subtitle, or Problem statement as a named goal.
findings.finding: The plan bundles three substantial features beyond its stated headline — (1) execution-phase tracking with a 0.80 priority floor, (2) a new require_plan_review_for_close gate, and (3) a specialized prerequisite-consistency LLM criterion — none of which are signaled by the title 'Keep plan-review attestations current across relationships and execution' or the subtitle 'Invalidate downstream plan reviews after upstream plan-material changes'. An agent reading only the title would not know these major mechanisms are included, and reviewers cannot easily separate the pin-currency work from the close-gate or execution-phase work when scoping, prioritizing, or rolling back.
findings.location: Title / 'Settled design' sections: 'Execution reviews and close gate', 'Prerequisite consistency review'
scenarios: An agent tasked with implementing only 'keeping attestations current across relationships' could omit the close gate and execution-phase features, or implement them differently, based on the title alone.
A reviewer scoping the rollback path for pin enforcement might not realize they also need to disable require_plan_review_for_close and unwind the execution-phase projection.
The 'and execution' phrase in the title is too terse to signal the full scope of the execution-phase mechanism, priority-floor policy, or the new LLM prerequisite-consistency criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: Retitle to something like 'Extend plan-review attestations with relationship pins, execution-phase tracking, and prerequisite-consistency enforcement' and add one sentence per feature area to the Problem section explaining why each is needed and how they relate to the core invalidation goal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that retry exhaustion maps to an existing verdict type (INDETERMINATE is the correct choice, consistent with 'any indeterminate record yields an unsigned INDETERMINATE review') rather than introducing a new 'retryable' result type.
evidence: Plan phrase: 'exhaustion returns a retryable unsigned result'
Codebase: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py uses verdict='PASS'|'BLOCK'|'INDETERMINATE'
Codebase: src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_verdict.schema.json — verdict is schema-validated
INDETERMINATE already covers 'evaluation or attribution cannot complete' per the plan's own language in 'Prerequisite consistency review'
findings.finding: The plan says retry exhaustion in the pre-sign transaction 'returns a retryable unsigned result', but does not map this to an existing verdict type. The plan-review system has three defined verdict types: PASS, BLOCK, and INDETERMINATE. 'Retryable unsigned' is not a recognized type and could cause schema validation failures or diverge from the `plan_review_verdict` schema contract.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins: 'exhaustion returns a retryable unsigned result'
scenarios: Implementer creates a fourth verdict type 'RETRY_EXHAUSTED' that fails schema validation
Implementer returns INDETERMINATE (correct) but the plan's language implies a distinct type, causing review-phase confusion about when INDETERMINATE is appropriate
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'exhaustion returns a retryable unsigned result' with 'exhaustion returns an unsigned INDETERMINATE result (consistent with the indeterminate disposition rule above), with a coverage note indicating retry-exhausted as the indeterminate reason'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the existing `packing_spot_eval` / `parity.container_fidelity_report` infrastructure can accommodate the 3-disposition `{consistent, finding, indeterminate}` prerequisite schema, or explicitly scope the required adaptations (new parity labels, new baseline fixtures, adapted recall/false-acceptance definitions) as named deliverables.
evidence: 'Packed behavior uses the existing whole-child bin-packing pattern and is release-gated against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline: packed recall must be no lower, packed false acceptance no higher, every prerequisite must receive a record, and attribution errors must be zero.'

src/rebar/llm/plan_review/fidelity_spot_eval.py lines 195–268: `packing_spot_eval` is built around `passes.pass1_container` (G3/G4 criteria only) and `parity.container_fidelity_report` which measures `block`/`advisory` decisions with gold `block`/`advisory` labels.

The prerequisite coverage record schema is `{prerequisite_id, disposition, findings}` with dispositions `consistent`/`finding`/`indeterminate` — none of these map to the existing `block`/`advisory` parity labels without adaptation.
ABSENCE: No plan statement that the parity infrastructure will be extended or adapted for the new disposition schema.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Packed behavior uses the existing whole-child bin-packing pattern' for prerequisite evaluation and requires a 'release gate against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline,' but the existing bin-packing infrastructure (`fidelity_spot_eval.packing_spot_eval`, `passes.pass1_container`, `parity.container_fidelity_report`) is purpose-built for the child G3/G4 pass/fail schema — it produces per-child `block`/`advisory` decisions, not the new three-disposition `{consistent, finding, indeterminate}` prerequisite coverage records. The plan does not verify that the existing parity machinery maps onto the prerequisite schema, nor that an adapted gold baseline (with `consistent`/`finding`/`indeterminate` ground-truth labels) can be built from the existing `packing_spot_eval` infrastructure. This is prior art reuse claimed without verifying capability fit.
findings.location: Settled design § Prerequisite consistency review — 'Packed behavior uses the existing whole-child bin-packing pattern and is release-gated against a one-prerequisite-per-call gold baseline'
scenarios: An implementor re-uses `packing_spot_eval` as-is but finds the `consistent`/`finding`/`indeterminate` outputs cannot be passed to `parity.container_fidelity_report`, requiring a new fidelity eval to be written from scratch — invalidating the 'uses existing pattern' claim.

The gold baseline is defined using `block`/`advisory` proxies for `finding`/`consistent`, silently mapping `indeterminate` to `advisory`, which mis-measures packing fidelity for the most complex case (the one that matters for certification).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a formal JSON schema for the prerequisite coverage record (`{prerequisite_id, disposition, findings}`) co-located with the other plan-review schemas, including constraints that `consistent` requires `findings: []` and `finding` requires `findings` non-empty with each element carrying a `prerequisite_id` attribution field.
evidence: Pass 1 returns exactly one coverage record per canonical prerequisite: {prerequisite_id, disposition, findings}, where disposition is consistent, finding, or indeterminate; consistent requires an empty findings list
No schema file for prerequisite coverage record exists in src/rebar/schemas/ (verified by directory listing — no prerequisite_coverage or similar schema present)
The existing plan_review_verdict.schema.json has no prerequisite_coverage array or prerequisite_id field
findings.finding: The prerequisite-criterion LLM output schema (`{prerequisite_id, disposition, findings}`) is referenced throughout the 'Prerequisite consistency review' section but never formally defined: the plan names the three fields and their types verbally, but provides no JSON/structural schema, no constraint on `findings` content when `disposition` is `consistent` (must findings be empty? the plan says 'consistent requires an empty findings list' only in prose), and no machine-checkable contract the verifier or Pass 2 can validate against.
findings.location: Settled design → Prerequisite consistency review (Pass 1 / Pass 2 paragraphs)
scenarios: An LLM returns a `consistent` record with a non-empty `findings` list — no schema enforcement catches it and it silently passes as consistent.
An implementer defines the coverage record differently across Pass 1 and Pass 2, causing attribution mismatches that the plan provides no machine-checkable guard against.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `plan_review_prerequisite_coverage.schema.json` to `src/rebar/schemas/` specifying: `{prerequisite_id: string, disposition: {enum: ['consistent', 'finding', 'indeterminate']}, findings: array, reason?: string}` with an `if/then` constraint enforcing `findings` is empty when `disposition` is `consistent` and non-empty when `disposition` is `finding`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the output schema for the prerequisite-criterion's Pass 2 verifier — either extend `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json` with a `prerequisite_verifications` field or define a separate schema — and describe how upheld/not-upheld prerequisite findings map to the existing blocking/dropped/indeterminate buckets in Pass 3.
evidence: 'Pass 2 receives the same subject/prerequisite map and verifies substance and prerequisite_id attribution. Only an upheld finding record can proceed to normal Pass 3 and the execution floor; unattributed or cross-ID findings cannot block.'
Existing plan_review_verify_inputs_output.schema.json uses index-keyed verifications with no prerequisite_id field
plan_review_decide_input.schema.json accepts `verifications: [{index, severity_attributes, binary}]` — no field for prerequisite attribution
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the prerequisite-criterion's three-disposition output integrates with the existing Pass 2 verifier schema. The existing `plan_review_verify_inputs_output.schema.json` routes findings by global index and yields binary `binary` verifications; the prerequisite coverage records use `prerequisite_id` attribution. The plan says 'Only an upheld finding record can proceed to normal Pass 3' but never defines the schema shape that carries 'upheld' vs 'not-upheld' from the prerequisite-specific verifier into the standard decide step, leaving a processing-rule/schema co-location gap.
findings.location: Settled design → Prerequisite consistency review (Pass 2 paragraph)
scenarios: Implementer must decide whether prerequisite findings go through the existing decide step (schema mismatch on prerequisite_id) or a separate decide step (not described), silently choosing wrong path.
A cross-ID finding that Pass 2 does not 'uphold' must still appear somewhere — the plan doesn't say whether it goes to dropped, advisory, or is silently discarded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the context-limit fallback protocol for prerequisite batching: define the failure signal (e.g., a token-budget pre-check or a specific model error class), the bin-reduction step (e.g., halve the bin or remove the largest item), and the terminal condition (minimum bin size = 1, then too-large remediation if subject alone exceeds the window).
evidence: 'Context-limit failures retry with smaller bins. Subject plus one oversized prerequisite yields the existing too-large remediation.'
'Multiple prerequisite evaluations may share a call when whole prerequisite blocks fit within existing context headroom. Batching is an optimization only.'
No definition of the bin-reduction algorithm or the trigger signal for fallback
findings.finding: The fallback decision rule for switching from packed-batch to single-prerequisite evaluation is underspecified as a processing protocol. The plan says 'context-limit failures retry with smaller bins' but does not define: (a) what signal constitutes a 'context-limit failure' (token-limit error from the model? a pre-call budget check?), (b) the bin-reduction algorithm (halve? remove one?), or (c) at what bin size the fallback to one-per-call is triggered. The existing bin-packing pattern in the plan review workflow (plan_review_verify_inputs_output.schema.json) uses a 'token-budgeted splits' approach, but no analogous budget-check mechanism is described for prerequisite evaluation.
findings.location: Settled design → Prerequisite consistency review (batching and context-limit paragraph)
scenarios: A model returns a generic LLM error (not a context-limit error) on a packed call — the retry protocol misclassifies and keeps reducing bin size instead of escalating.
A 'smaller bins' retry produces another context-limit failure — the plan doesn't specify whether this loops, falls through to one-per-call, or yields indeterminate.

findings.finding: The two new manifest lines `review-phase:` and `priority-floor:` are referenced throughout the plan but their insertion position within `build_manifest()`'s deterministic line order is never specified. The existing implementation has a fixed sequence (plan-review → ticket → material → model → runner → blocking → advisory → rebar-version → regver → disabled_builtins → refreshed-from → verified_at_sha → dep-lines). An executor must guess placement; signing and verification require byte-identical line order, so any divergence produces a permanently broken signature.
findings.location: Settled design → Signed plan-material pins; Settled design → Execution reviews and close gate; build_manifest() in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/manifest.py lines 190-224
findings.suggested_fix: Insert `review-phase:` immediately after `advisory:`, `priority-floor:` (when present) immediately after `review-phase:`, and `plan-material-pin:` lines (sorted by role then canonical ID) immediately before `dep` lines. Add `manifest_review_phase()`, `manifest_priority_floor()`, and `manifest_pins()` parser functions in manifest.py following the existing pattern.

findings.finding: The boundary between an 'unresolvable' prerequisite (which triggers all-or-nothing pin-collection failure and blocks signing) and a 'readable-but-indeterminate' prerequisite (which allows signing to proceed with an indeterminate disposition) is never formally specified. The plan uses both terms without defining what makes a target transition from one category to the other, leaving the fallback decision rule ambiguous.
findings.location: Settled design → Signed plan-material pins: 'unresolved, malformed, duplicate, or unreadable required targets stop the review'; 'The indeterminate prerequisite disposition exists only for a readable, successfully pinned target whose model evaluation or attribution cannot complete'

findings.finding: The plan describes a non-trivial bootstrap rule for pre-feature SNAPSHOTs ('bootstrap planning when its compiled status is open or idea and execution for any other status') and an associated reducer-cache version bump, but no acceptance criterion verifies this behavior. A wrong bootstrap mapping (e.g., an in-progress ticket bootstrapped as planning) would silently under-enforce the 0.80 floor on existing work.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate (bootstrap paragraph); Acceptance Criteria (no covering line)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Pre-feature SNAPSHOTs without plan_review_phase are bootstrapped to planning for open/idea status and execution for all other compiled statuses, effective on first post-upgrade reduction.'

findings.finding: The parent's settled design specifies a bootstrap rule for pre-feature snapshots missing plan_review_phase: 'bootstrap planning when its compiled status is open or idea and execution for any other status. A reducer-cache version bump makes the rule apply on first post-upgrade reduction.' No acceptance criterion in 5967-4476-7f9f-442d (the child that owns plan_review_phase) explicitly covers this bootstrap rule or the cache-version bump mechanism. The child's AC4 ('Compaction snapshots preserve the projection') is a forward-only property and does not verify backward-compatibility bootstrapping.
findings.location: child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d, Acceptance Criteria (missing bootstrap AC); parent Settled Design section 'Execution reviews and close gate'
findings.suggested_fix: Add two ACs: 'Pre-feature snapshots without plan_review_phase bootstrap to planning when compiled status is open or idea, and execution otherwise.' and 'A reducer-cache version bump causes first post-upgrade reduction to apply the bootstrap rule idempotently.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
